1: Ahaz was 20 and reigned 16 years in Jerusalem. He did not do right in YHWH's eyes, 'as his father David had done.' The Davidic standard is explicitly cited — and explicitly violated. Ahaz represents the anti-David: each of David's defining characteristics (seeking God, covenant loyalty, building toward the temple) is negated by Ahaz's reign. Christ is the fulfillment of the Davidic ideal: 'the son of David' (Matthew 1:1) who recapitulates all that David sought and actualizes it.
2: Ahaz walked in the ways of the kings of Israel; he also made metal images for the Baals. The Baal-apostasy in the southern kingdom — adopting the sins of the north that led to its fall. Christ is the antithesis of the idol-walker: 'No one knows the Father except the Son' (Matthew 11:27); the one who reveals the true character of God is the alternative to every Baal — every power misnamed as divine.
3: He burned incense in the Valley of the Son of Hinnom (gêʾ-hinnōm) and burned his children in the fire, following the detestable practices of the nations. The Valley of Hinnom — Gehenna — the historical site of child-sacrifice, which becomes the NT term for the place of eschatological judgment. Christ speaks of Gehenna as the place to be feared (Matthew 10:28: 'fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell'). He came 'to destroy the works of the devil' (1 John 3:8), including the demonic child-sacrifice system Ahaz practiced.
4: He sacrificed and burned incense on the high places, on the hills, and under every green tree. The multiplication of unauthorized worship sites — the decentralized idolatry that the Deuteronomic reform program aimed to eliminate. Christ calls worshippers away from all localized idolatry: 'the true worshippers will worship the Father in spirit and truth' (John 4:23) — not tied to any hill or tree but to the one who is himself the way to the Father.
5: YHWH gave Ahaz into the hand of the king of Aram, who struck him and took captive a great number. Also into the hand of the king of Israel, who struck him with a great blow. The double defeat by Aram and Israel — the covenant curse of being given into the hand of enemies (Deuteronomy 28:25). Christ bore this 'given into the hand' formula on behalf of his people: 'the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men' (Matthew 17:22) — the representative king experiencing the covenant curse so the people need not.
6: Pekah son of Remaliah killed 120,000 valiant men in Judah in one day, because they had forsaken YHWH. The scale of covenant-judgment catastrophe. The Chronicler assigns causation explicitly: apostasy → abandonment by YHWH → military slaughter. Christ absorbs the ultimate apostasy-penalty: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46) — the cry of divine abandonment that the covenant curses of Deut 28 threatened, experienced by the one who had not forsaken God.
7: Zichri, a mighty man of Ephraim, killed the king's son Maaseiah, Azrikam the commander of the palace, and Elkanah the second in command to the king. The royal family and command structure decimated. In Christ's death the disciples who constituted his inner circle were scattered (Mark 14:50); but his resurrection reestablishes and commissions them (John 20:21-22).
8: The men of Israel took captive 200,000 of their brothers' wives, sons, and daughters, and also took much plunder. The massive captive-taking — brothers enslaving brothers in the covenant family. Christ comes to proclaim 'liberty to captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed' (Luke 4:18) — the Isaiah 61 mission that reverses the captive-taking pattern of Ahaz's day.
9: Oded the prophet of YHWH met the army of Samaria and said: 'The LORD, the God of your fathers, was angry with Judah, so he gave them into your hand; but you have killed them in a rage that has reached up to heaven.' The northern prophet intercedes across the political divide. Christ is the ultimate cross-boundary intercessor: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34) — intercession at the moment of the greatest injustice.
10: The captors propose to hold the Judahites as male and female slaves. Oded counters: 'are there not sins of your own against the LORD your God?' The tu quoque of the prophet — the captors are themselves guilty. Paul makes the same move in Romans 2:1: 'Therefore you have no excuse, O man, every one of you who judges. For in passing judgment on another you condemn yourself.' Christ alone has no sins of his own against the Father and so stands as the sole qualified judge (John 8:46: 'which one of you convicts me of sin?').
11: 'Now hear me, and send back the captives whom you have taken from your brothers, for the fierce wrath of the LORD is upon you.' The prophetic call to release — the Jubilee logic applied across political lines. Christ's mission is the ultimate release: 'if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed' (John 8:36). The captive-releasing mandate of the OT prophets finds its fulfillment in the one who was himself taken captive (arrested) to secure the permanent release of others.
12: Certain chiefs of Ephraim stood up against those returning from battle: Azariah son of Johanan, Berechiah son of Meshillemoth, Jehizkiah son of Shallum, and Amasa son of Hadlai. Named individuals who take a stand against injustice from within the northern tribe. Ordinary people of faith who act against the grain of their political moment — a recurring pattern in the biblical narrative; Christ's own family of disciples is similarly constituted from unlikely individuals who respond to the Spirit's prompting.
13: 'You shall not bring the captives in here, for you propose to bring upon us guilt against the LORD in addition to our present sins and guilt. For our guilt is already great, and there is fierce anger against Israel.' The covenant-awareness of accumulated guilt — the leaders of Ephraim understand that adding to sin compounds judgment. Christ comes to deal with accumulated guilt: 'the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29) — not adding to guilt but removing it in a single comprehensive act.
14: The armed men left the captives and the plunder before the princes and all the assembly. The response to the prophetic and community moral pressure — the armed men stand down. The coercive power yields to the word. Christ's word also disarms: when he said 'I am he,' they drew back and fell to the ground (John 18:6) — the power of his self-disclosure exceeds any military capability.
15: The named men rose and took the captives, and from the plunder they clothed those who were naked, gave them sandals, fed them, gave them drink, anointed them with oil, carried those who were faint on donkeys, and brought them to Jericho. The seven-act compassion chain toward enemy captives is the OT's closest narrative parallel to the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:33-35): clothing, sandals, food, drink, anointing, transport — the same acts, the same endpoint (Jericho), the same unexpected agents of compassion. Christ embodies this compassion absolutely: 'I was naked and you clothed me... I was sick and you visited me, I was in prison and you came to me' (Matthew 25:35-36).
16: At that time King Ahaz sent to the king of Assyria for help. The appeal to empire for security against coalition threat — the Ahaz solution. Isaiah specifically condemns this appeal and offers the Immanuel sign instead (Isaiah 7:9-14). Christ is the Immanuel that Ahaz refused: 'God with us' (Matthew 1:23) is the divine help that does not require Assyrian alliance.
17: The Edomites had come again and struck Judah and carried away captives. Multiple simultaneous threats pressing on Judah. Christ's people also face multi-front pressure: 'in this world you will have tribulation' (John 16:33). But the promise continues: 'I have overcome the world' — the comprehensive victory that addresses simultaneous threats from every direction.
18: The Philistines had raided the cities in the Shephelah and the Negeb of Judah, and had taken Beth-shemesh, Aijalon, Gederoth, Soco with its villages, Timnah with its villages, and Gimzo with its villages. The piecemeal loss of lowland territories — Judah's geographic shrinkage under Ahaz. Under Christ, the kingdom expands rather than shrinks: 'the kingdom of heaven is like a grain of mustard seed that a man took and sowed in his field. It is the smallest of all seeds, but when it has grown it is larger than all the garden plants' (Matthew 13:31-32). The representative king's faithfulness expands; the representative king's unfaithfulness contracts.
19: YHWH humbled Judah because of Ahaz king of Israel, for he had made Judah act sinfully and had been very unfaithful to the LORD. The representative king principle — the whole nation is humbled because of one king's apostasy. Christ is the positive counterpart: his obedience and righteousness are credited to the whole people — 'for as by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous' (Romans 5:19).
20: Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria came to him but afflicted him instead of strengthening him. The purchased alliance fails — the imperial power takes tribute but does not deliver on the implicit promise of protection. Christ delivers on every promise: 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' (Hebrews 13:5, citing Deuteronomy 31:6). The empire that takes and does not give is the anti-type of the king who gives and does not take.
21: Ahaz took from the house of YHWH, the house of the king, and the princes, and gave tribute to the king of Assyria; but it did not help him. The stripping of the temple to pay Assyria — using what was devoted to YHWH to secure pagan political alliance. Christ is stripped at the cross (his garments divided: Mark 15:24), but his stripping is not the depletion of holy resources for political survival — it is the willing outpouring of self for the salvation of others.
22: In the time of his distress, King Ahaz became yet more faithless to YHWH — this was that King Ahaz. The epitaphic formula: distress → more faithlessness. The opposite of the covenant pattern (distress → cry → repentance → restoration). Manasseh will reverse this pattern in 2 Chronicles 33 (distress → humility → restoration). Christ under distress is the ultimate alternative: 'who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame' (Hebrews 12:2) — suffering transformed into perseverance, not into faithlessness.
23: Ahaz sacrificed to the gods of Damascus, which had defeated him, saying 'Because the gods of the kings of Aram helped them, I will sacrifice to them that they may help me.' The logic of military outcome determining religious allegiance — the gods of the winners must be powerful. Christ exposes the bankruptcy of this logic: 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18) after a death that looked like defeat. The resurrection overturns the outcome-as-divine-verdict equation.
24: Ahaz gathered together the vessels of the house of God and cut them in pieces; he also shut the doors of the house of YHWH and made himself altars at every corner in Jerusalem. The double act: destroying the temple vessels and shutting the temple — the most comprehensive assault on covenant worship in the southern kingdom's history. Christ does the opposite: 'something greater than the temple is here' (Matthew 12:6), and 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us' (Hebrews 10:19-20). What Ahaz shut, Christ opens permanently.
25: In every city of Judah Ahaz made high places to burn incense to other gods, provoking YHWH the God of his fathers to anger. The multiplied altars replacing the one temple — the decentralization of false worship at the moment when the legitimate worship was shut. Christ calls back from scattered idolatry to himself: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6) — the singular path that replaces the many altars on every corner.
26: The rest of his acts and all his ways, from first to last, are written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel. The historical record of Ahaz's reign. The Chronicler gives him no positive final notation — the acts written are the acts of an apostate. Christ's acts are written for a different purpose: 'these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name' (John 20:31). The record written for judgment becomes, in the NT, the record written for faith and life.
27: Ahaz slept with his fathers and they buried him in the city of Jerusalem — not in the tombs of the kings of Israel. The final exclusion: denied burial in the royal tombs. The worst king of the southern kingdom is excluded from his own heritage at death. Christ was buried in another's tomb (Matthew 27:60), excluded from any earthly claim to burial property — 'foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (Matthew 8:20) — yet his resurrection transforms the borrowed tomb into the site of the greatest victory in human history.
